speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's not married yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet. Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Children?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just married three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Take your time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It's better earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's better earlier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know better. Your wife working?

speaker: interviewer
text: My wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is she working?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Stand by. Slate it, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. Its April 2, 1995. I'm speaking to Gregory Leyfell. The interviewer is Pamela Goodman. The city is Jamaica, New York. And the country is the United States. And we're speaking in English. Mr. Leyfell, you want to talk a little bit about your life here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not easy to adjust to-- I was not a youngster anymore. And I didn't have any-- any profession that I could-- somebody who was a shoemaker or a tailor or any-- any definite, you know, [INAUDIBLE]. I was-- so I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started, I mean, I knew a friend here who-- whom I-- in Poland during the war. And he was a jeweler, was a jeweler. And when I came here, he came from-- through Germany, like a refugee, came to the United States with a wife and a son. And he became-- he was very hardworking, energetic and became for himself and work for himself and built himself up as a jeweler. And then when I started to look for a job, he offered me to-- to work for him, you know. And-- but unfortunately, I'm not a jeweler. So he used me more like a salesman. So I started to go out and sell to jeweler, to stores, to jewelry stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- he lived in Brooklyn. And I stayed with him for the first almost a year. And then I moved to-- to Queens here. And then I started to-- and it was very hard, too. Because I didn't know the trade. And I didn't-- I didn't know the people. I didn't know. So I remember it was one situation quite a-- an amusing situation. I went to a jewelry store. I went to jewelry stores to try to sell to them. So I went to Madison Avenue in New York. There was a nice jewelry store, [INAUDIBLE]. The name was [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walked into the store and asked the-- who's the buyer? Who's the buyer? And the guy said, I am the buyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him, I asked to see Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, I'm afraid you wouldn't like to see Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. He died five years ago. But see the people who were in that trade, they knew about it. I didn't know anybody, you know. So I had-- I had to use-- and then I went there was a big jewelry and gallery [INAUDIBLE] on 5th Avenue and 55th Street. Also, I didn't know them. But somehow, I came in. And well, I-- to give you an idea, I have here a friend who was a biochemist. He came from Poland, too. And when I told him my-- my life during the occupation and here, and he told me you survived thanks to your personality, he told me. You survived thanks to your personality back in under occupational and here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think it was about your personality that helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Somehow somehow I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to describe it. I don't know. You know, some people-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you judge. I don't know, so this helped me a lot. And then I started to-- to teach Russian. Oh, I forgot about that. And I started, I went, and I passed the test on the Board of Education. And I was teaching Russian for quite-- about six, seven years, I was teaching Russian. And then I gave up Russian, because I had to do it in the evening. And it was too tiresome. And-- and you-- I was getting paid better as a salesman than a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave up teaching. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how I survived until now. And he died. And after he died-- The man who owned-- who-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: The son somehow-- he left too much money, I suppose. So the son didn't need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: The jewelry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the jeweler that I worked for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. So it was-- so the business was closed. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I worked since 19-- since 1951 until-- until 1983. Well, it's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a jewelry salesman all that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But, unfortunately, I'm not a jeweler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I am an old man. And-- and I have bad vision, bad eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all together, I'm not-- not in good shape, not in good form. Struggling--

speaker: interviewer
text: Struggling.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any family here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have some cousins, yeah, some cousins, yeah. I have a cousins in Boston, who is very nice, a doctor. And-- he's very nice to my brother. My brother lives there. He's very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother lives--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my brother, by the way, came-- came here much later, to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, how did he come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, [INAUDIBLE] This, I can tell you. Yes, here. And he came here. It was not easy to get the-- the visa. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he-- he-- when we last left him, he was in [INAUDIBLE]. How did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, later he came back to Poland. He was also in the diplomatic service. And then later, he became-- he's very educated. He became an editor. But later, when Poland became the situation very bad for Jews, people left. It was very hard to-- he left. But it was very hard to get the visa here to get to the United States. In this-- in this case, I helped. Because I was here. And I went to the State Department, who-- who are very, very nice to me. And this helped to bring him here. When he came here, he got-- got a job. But he's well-educated. And he got this job at the MIT as the librarian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a very good lucky strike [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel having family here, having close family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It's very good. It's very good. And they-- they live in Cambridge. So I used to go more often by train, the shuttle. But now it is harder to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he called me. I called him about this now, tell him about this interview. His wife was teaching Polish at Harvard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for some time. But now, they are retired. [INAUDIBLE] retired. Because they, they have their own house not far-- not far from Harvard. You know, Harvard Square? You know? Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I they good friends-- friends there. We have friends there. And Cambridge is a nice city, a nice town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for retiring. It's not so big like New York, you know. It's easy. You have all the universities. They have a lot of activities there, a lot of theater and concerts and lectures. It's a good city for retiring.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the pictures. You want to do pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to do those. I want to ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that's more reflective in terms of your experiences during the war. You've had many close calls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had several people who cared for you and watched.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, who jeopardized their lives. If they were caught, they would be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about it, when you look back on it, when you reflect on all this, what sense do you make of all this? What-- what comes into your mind when you think about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, I had-- I had a quarrel with God. That if you are there, how can you allow to things like this happening? I never got answer. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe? Do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know how describe it. I believe there is, as I call, it the factor in life that you cannot explain, you cannot-- that logic-- logic fails. A situation like mine, like [INAUDIBLE] told me that the Holy Ghost is taking care of me, you know. There are situations you cannot-- cannot understand, you never understand. So you have to-- you'll have to think there is something-- something there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Everybody-- ?] the main thing is a human being should be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] should be understanding for another human being. This is the main thing. But somehow it doesn't go like this. They're killing-- people kill each other all over the world nowadays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we are lucky we are here. Right? Yes. Is there anything else that pertains to your war experience that you would like to say, any stories that you might have left out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I can tell you that hunger is a terrible thing. What hunger can-- can do to people is unbelievable, unbelievable. I saw a son-- a son coming up. And his father was dying. And he was so hungry, that he didn't-- he didn't even look at his father dying. And he was-- there was something, a little soup in a pot staying there for [INAUDIBLE]. And he was so hungry. Hunger is a terrible thing, terrible thing. I was-- I was-- when we were hiding in this [INAUDIBLE]. I-- we didn't have almost a whole week, really [INAUDIBLE] without any food, without anything. You can see me in the picture of how much I weighed at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you think about the-- the story of the son who didn't see his dying father, he only saw the food--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the man who left his wife and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the-- the instinct for self-preservation is there. There are stories-- I-- this-- this is the one I witnessed. But I heard about stories that the Germans asked to give up children. And there was a story about my hometown. I heard about it. I hope it's not right, that one gave up her baby. And she would hope that she will survive or something. This what I-- I didn't witness it. I just heard about [INAUDIBLE] it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there children in your family that didn't survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Oh, this one you have to give me back this, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, my-- I had a-- a nephew, who-- who-- who was to me, like a son, very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ionia. Ionia. You have the picture there as a baby. And then my other sister had a-- a boy about five, six years old. And then my-- my brother had a boy who was probably by the time at the time about seven years old. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't even have picture. I don't. The only picture I found here, because my cousin had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. This might be a good time to show your pictures. Let's do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is only the coffin. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe for us [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, this is Maria Nikitichna. This is the Russian woman who saved my life. The initiative [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to save my life came from her. I told her, whatever happened to the older Jews let happen to me. But she insisted. And she jeopardized her own life and everything to save my life. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] She died in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her husband sent me this picture taken at the funeral. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can never forget her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Can I talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture was taken immediate soon after my liberation. It was in the summer of 19-- March or April, 1944. There are two women there, sisters, whose-- whose house-- I lived in their house. And they helped me all the time. And thanks to them, I really survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to say their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there are-- then there are two Russian officers, who helped us with supplying food when they came in and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say the name of the two sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The two sisters, who are-- one was Zoia, Russian, of course. And the other was Liza. They were very religious. When somebody once tried to convince her that they shouldn't keep me, because the Germans will-- will kill them and burn the house, and then she said, I believe in God. And if it is meant for me to survive, I'll survive with [INAUDIBLE] here. And the other, Zoia, was the one who during the Nazi occupation she came in with two SS men who-- to pick up their radio [INAUDIBLE], which [? Leonid ?] was repairing. And somehow they didn't know I was Jewish. And I didn't give myself away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I ask her why-- how could you do it? How could you bring in two SS men into my room and not telling me about it? And she said to me, [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't understand. Why should they know that you are Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she behaved so naturally that they couldn't have an idea. They didn't say, introduce themselves. And I was the-- the interpreter. And everything went smooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you, on the right side there? That's you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm sure, you know who I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bottom, the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Show it to me. I'll show you. I cannot see from there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah. This is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is-- which are the women? Can you tell the different between the two women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Zoia and which one is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Zoia is to the-- to the left there. No, no, no there. Zoia is this. And the other standing is Liza.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. To the right standing behind her [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Liza, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other is Zoia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my nephew who-- who was, to me, like a son or like something very precious. I couldn't think that I'll survive after he-- he was killed. This picture was taken when he was a small child. And it was mailed to a cousin in Chicago. That's where I got this picture. Because, otherwise, I don't have any pictures of my family. I don't have any.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, Ionia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ionia.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a-- a fantastic, talented, talented-- when was killed, he was graduated high school. And he was very talented. And it's very hard each time to-- to look at the picture. Cannot-- it's not easy to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the personnel of the Polish Embassy in Turkey in Ankara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Embassy there is a very beautiful building with a big-- big orchard. And all-- this was taken in 19-- in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's find where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah I am there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: There. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the occasion of this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this occasion must have been a-- a-- a holiday or something. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is good for me almost. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is also a picture of-- of the-- the personnel of the Polish Embassy in Turkey. The-- the occasion, I won't remember exactly the occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was a-- it was-- oh, that was in 1947, the beginning of 1947. It was a friendly group. It's a-- a nice souvenir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people all knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: These people all knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my brother, Alexander, and his wife, Anita. This was taken, I think, in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Switzerland or France. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was probably in the 1950s. I don't-- I couldn't tell exactly the year-- the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was still working for the Polish Embassy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. And now, they live-- they live in Cambridge now. My brother was associated with MIT and Harvard Russian school. And my sister was teaching Russian at Harvard. Can I talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, this is my brother, Alexander. We are two-- the only two survivors of our big family. He lives in Cambridge, as I said before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever talk to Alexander about the war at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk to each other and see each other. And I-- I-- I very seldom, I very seldom talk about my past, my family, my-- I very seldom. He is more-- because he was-- he was not under the Nazi occupation. Maybe because of this, he rather likes to-- to talk about the past, about the hometown, Ostrog, and his years in gymnasium and the university. He goes back to the past very often. And he's still very much interested in anything about the country, about the city we were born to. I, rather, maybe it's this- this instinct of self-preservation, I have been trying to not to talk about it, to not to go back to this sad event.

speaker: interviewer
text: This has been hard for you to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. It is too hard for me to to-- to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you for-- for doing this, difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thank you. I thank you very much, really. You worked hard. You worked hard. Thank you very much. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Leyfell. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And-- I don't know. Somebody in the-- maybe in the future will look at this and read this. I think that it's important that people shouldn't forget the past. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you, too, and you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
